title: List Comprehensions Practice
instructions: Practice creating lists using comprehensions with transformations, filters, and conditions.
hints: Use comprehension with filter: [x**2 for x in numbers if x % 2 == 0]
Filter by length: [len(w) for w in words if len(w) >= min_length]
Nested comprehension: [num for row in matrix for num in row]
Nested list comprehension: [[0 for _ in range(cols)] for _ in range(rows)]

testCases.description: squares_of_evens should return squares of only the even numbers
squares_of_evens should return empty list when no evens exist
testCases.validation: assert squares_of_evens([1, 3, 5]) == [], 'squares_of_evens with all odds should return []'

testCases.description: word_lengths should return lengths of words meeting minimum length
testCases.validation: assert word_lengths(['hi', 'hello', 'hey', 'howdy'], 4) == [5, 5], 'word_lengths with min_length=4 should filter short words'

testCases.description: flatten_matrix should convert a 2D list into a 1D list
create_grid should create a 2D grid of zeros with correct dimensions
testCases.validation: assert create_grid(2, 3) == [[0, 0, 0], [0, 0, 0]], 'create_grid(2,3) should return 2x3 grid of zeros'

testCases.description: create_grid rows should be independent (not shared references)
testCases.validation: g = create_grid(2, 2); g[0][0] = 1; assert g[1][0] == 0, 'Grid rows should be independent lists'

description: Practice Python list comprehensions for concise list creation. Build filtered transformations (squares of evens), conditional length extraction, nested comprehensions to flatten 2D matrices, and nested list comprehensions to construct grids. List comprehensions are a powerful Pythonic alternative to traditional for-loop patterns.
